request.question: Did Mala Mala or Train Life come first?

title: Kapana (grilled meat)
Martin Ralph
Mala Mala (film)
Music of Anguilla
Federal Power Act
When Things of the Spirit Come First
Swing (Australian politics)
Mala vita
St Philip Howard Catholic Voluntary Academy
Train Life
sentences: Kapana Grilled Beef is a way of preparing raw meat, typically beef in Namibia by grilling it on open fire.
The grilled meat is often sold at open market by young and small business people; it is cut into small pieces and grilled and sold while it's cooking.
People from all walks of life come and enjoy the meat.
There are often many people selling it at one place, so the price is always negotiable.

Kapana business has contributed in a small way to the economic development of the country by giving unemployed young people an opportunity to start their business with a very low capital and look after their family.
Kapana in Namibia is popular in Windhoek Katutura area and people from surrounding locations visit Katutura to eat Kapana.
Martin Ralph (born 18 January 1960) is an Australian sprint canoeist who competed in the mid-1980s.
At the 1984 Summer Olympics in Los Angeles, he was eliminated in the repechages of the K-2 500 m event.
Subsequently, he began to coach with the VIS(Victorian Institute of Sport) and independently.
Martin received the Australian Sports Medal from the Prime Minister and the Governor General for sporting achievement in the year 2000.
Often asked if he won a gold medal Martin replies, "I didn't come first and I didn't come last but I got a real good look at the bloke who did!"
Mala Mala is a 2014 Puerto Rican documentary film directed by Antonio Santini and Dan Sickles, starring Jason "April" Carrión, Samantha Close and Ivana Fred.
This film shows several stories of the transgender community in Puerto Rico, including April Carrion, well-known drag queen who participated in the reality show RuPaul's Drag Race.

Mala Mala also includes the historic victory of the LGBT community with the approval and signature of Law 238-2014 (in Puerto Rico), which prevents discrimination in employment based on sexual orientation and/or gender identity.
Mala Mala has been presented in festivals around the world, including London, Ukraine, Los Angeles, Austin, Costa Rica and Mexico.
In addition to schools such as The Boston Conservatory at Berklee, University of Pennsylvania, New York University and Harvard University.
The music of Anguilla is part of the Lesser Antillean music area.
The earliest people of the island were the Caribs and Arawaks, who arrived from South America.
English settlers from St Kitts and Irish people later colonised the island.
Unlike regional neighbours, however, the plantation system of agriculture that relied on chattel slavery never took root in Anguilla, causing a distinctly independent cultural makeup.

The most recent influences on Anguilla's musical life come from elsewhere in the Caribbean, especially the music of Trinidad and Tobago and Jamaica, as well as abroad, especially the music of the United States and the United Kingdom.

Anguilla's Rastafarian heritage has played a role in the island's music and culture and produced influential figures like activist Ijahnya Christian and Robert Athlyi Rogers, author of "The Holy Piby".
The Federal Power Act is a law appearing in Chapter 12 of Title 16 of the United States Code, entitled "Federal Regulation and Development of Power".

Enacted as the Federal Water Power Act on June 10, 1920, and amended many times since, its original purpose was to more effectively coordinate the development of hydroelectric projects in the United States.
Representative John J. Esch (R-Wisconsin) was the sponsor.

Prior to this time and despite federal control of navigable waters and the necessary congressional approval to construct such facilities, Congress had left the regulation of hydroelectric power to the individual states.

The first federal legislation broadly dealing with hydroelectric development regarded its competition with navigation usage; with the passage of the Rivers and Harbors Act of 1899 Congress made it illegal to dam navigable streams without a license (or permit) from them.
Until 1903, these congressional permits were given away on a 'first come first served' perpetual basis and controlled by the individual states.
This would lead to a long debate between competing private and public development interests, and culminate in the act's passage in 1920.
When Things of the Spirit Come First is Simone de Beauvoir's 'first' work of fiction.
After a number of false starts, in 1937 she submitted this collection of interlinked stories to a publisher.
But it was turned down by both Gallimard and Grasset.
It consists of five short stories which are weaved together in such a way that it to structurally similar to a more traditional novel.
The first, "Maurcelle", tells the story of the oldest of three siblings.
She marries an abusive artist.
The second, "Chantal", tells the story of a lycee philosophy teacher (like de Beauvoir).
She idealizes her life and becomes involved in the lives of her students but ultimately refuses to help them.
"Lisa" is the third and shortest story, about a girl who struggles to live a spiritual life while existing in a physical body.
"Anne", the fourth story, is the result of many of de Beauvoir's earlier attempts at writing.
It parallels the story of her friend Elisabeth Mabille (Zaza) who died soon after her mother refused to allow her to marry Maurice Merleau-Ponty.
The final story, "Marguarite" expresses the existential views that de Beauvoir herself believed that life itself should be experienced, rather than spirituality.
The term swing refers to the extent of change in voter support, typically from one election or opinion poll to another, expressed as a positive or negative percentage point.

For the Australian House of Representatives and the lower houses of the parliaments of all the states and territories except Tasmania and the ACT, Australia employs preferential voting in single-member constituencies.

Under the full-preference instant-runoff voting system, in each seat the candidate with the lowest vote is eliminated and their preferences are distributed, which is repeated until only two candidates remain.
While every seat has a two-candidate preferred (TCP) result, seats where the major parties have come first and second are commonly referred to as having a two-party-preferred (TPP) result.
The concept of "swing" in Australian elections is not simply a function of the difference between the votes of the two leading candidates, as it is in Britain.
To know the majority of any seat, and therefore the swing necessary for it to change hands, it is necessary to know the preferences of all the voters, regardless of their first preference votes.
It is not uncommon in Australia for candidates who have comfortable leads on the first count to fail to win the seat, because "preference flows" go against them.

Mala vita (Wretched Life) is an opera in three acts composed by Umberto Giordano to a libretto by Nicola Daspuro adapted from Salvatore Di Giacomo and Goffredo Cognetti's verismo play of the same name.
Giordano's first full-length opera, "Mala vita" premiered on 21 February 1892 at the Teatro Argentina.
It was subsequently performed in Naples, Vienna, Berlin and Milan, and other Italian cities over the next two years.
In 1897 a considerably re-worked and revised version under the title "Il voto" (The Vow) premiered in Milan.
Within a few years both versions had disappeared from the repertoire.
Amongst its rare modern revivals was the 2002 performance at the Teatro Umberto Giordano in Foggia which was recorded live and released on the Bongiovanni label.
St Philip Howard Catholic Voluntary Academy (formerly St Philip Howard Catholic School) is a Roman Catholic secondary school located in Glossop in northern Derbyshire.
It traditionally provides secondary education for Catholic school children in the Glossopdale and Longdendale valleys.
However, the school attracts applications from a wide variety of backgrounds and primary schools in Glossop.
The school has been established 52 years (as of 2013).
The current headteacher is Wendy Steciuk, who has held the post since September 2003.
The school attends a religious service at the nearby St Mary's Catholic Church at least once a term.
The school welcomes applications from all students, but makes clear that Catholic children come first in the selection process.
Traditionally, students come from Catholic primary schools around Glossop, Gamesley, Hadfield and Old Glossop.
However, students sometimes join the school from other non-religious schools in Glossop, Tintwistle and the Longdendale and Tameside areas.
Train Life is a 2006 documentary film about riders on Amtrak's Capitol Corridor.
It tells the story of life aboard the train through interviews with 12 commuters.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: Did Mala Mala or Train Life come first?
hop1_docs: Buddha chitta mala | Buddha chitta mala commonly referred as 'Bodhi mala', 'bodhi chitta mala' or 'buddha mala' is a string of mala beads that are made from the natural seeds from Buddha chitta plant. " Ziziphus budhensis", the scientific name for buddha chitta plant is a species of plant in the Rhamnaceae family endemic to the Timal region of Kavreplanchok in Central Nepal.  'Buddha chitta' or 'Ziziphus Budhensis' is clearly different from the species of 'Ziziphus' already known to grow in Nepal, and it did not match with the Ziziphus species reported to be found in India, Pakistan, Bhutan, Bangladesh, and China.

Mala (song) | "Mala" is the first single from Yolandita's 31st studio album, Mala.  This song was written by Maria Isabel Saavedra and produced by Jose Luis Pagan.  The Yolandita new single “Mala”, was released on October 6, 2008 on national radio.
Mala Mala Airport | Mala Mala Airport (IATA: AAM, ICAO: FAMD) is an airport near Mala Mala, South Africa.

Mala Kapela Tunnel | Mala Kapela Tunnel (Croatian: "Tunel Mala Kapela" ) is the most significant structure on the Croatian A1 motorway route.  As it is 5821 m long (northbound tube), Mala Kapela Tunnel is the longest tunnel in Croatia.  The tunnel is geographically located between the villages of Jezerane and Modruš (although it cannot be accessed from the villages directly), and between Ogulin and Brinje motorway interchanges.  The tunnel passes through the Mala Kapela mountain in the northeast-southwest direction.  The first tunnel tube through the Mala Kapela mountain was completed in 2005, and was used for both directions of traffic until the second tube was completed in 2009.

Via Mala (1961 film) | Via Mala is a 1961 West German drama film directed by Paul May and starring Gert Fröbe, Joachim Hansen and Christine Kaufmann.  It is an adaptation of the 1934 novel "Via Mala" by John Knittel, which had previously been made into a film in 1945.  After a tyrannical father in a small Swiss village is killed, almost everyone he knows comes under suspicion of having murdered him.
Mala Rodríguez | María Rodríguez Garrido, also known as La Mala, La Mala María, or Mala Rodríguez ("Mala", Spanish for "bad"), is a Latin Grammy Award-winning Spanish hip hop singer.

Mala Gente (song) | "Mala Gente" is a song written about Ashley Caruth, by Colombian singer-songwriter Juanes.  The song is the third radio single from his second studio album, "Un Día Normal".  It shows his frustration with his soon-to-be ex-girlfriend who he calls "mala gente" ("evil people" or "evil woman".  She tells him that their romance is now over, and she wants to be friends.  He believes her to be a liar, that she cannot be trusted, has played with his emotions and does not want to be friends.  That she will come to regret this breakup.  His prediction comes true.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Mala Mala: mentioned in "Mala Mala Airport" — snippet "Mala Mala, South Africa." (no release/publication info; place name only).
  - Train Life: not mentioned in the provided passages.
- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - It is unclear whether the question refers to works (films/songs/books) titled exactly "Mala Mala" and "Train Life" — passages do not show either as a dated work.
  - No release/publication years or work-type confirmations for either candidate in the passages.
- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - Need the release/publication year (or exact release date) for "Mala Mala" (the work intended).
  - Need the release/publication year (or exact release date) for "Train Life" (the work intended).
  - Need confirmation that both titles refer to comparable work types (e.g., both films) if relevant.
- retrieval_direction (priority order):
  1. Wikipedia page "Mala Mala (film)" or "Mala Mala" (documentary) — retrieve release year and type.
  2. Wikipedia page "Train Life" or "Train Life (film/song/book)" — retrieve release year and type.
  3. If exact pages not found, search queries: "Mala Mala release year documentary 'Mala Mala' Puerto Rico" and "Train Life release year 'Train Life' film".
  4. General web search: "Mala Mala film 2014" and "Train Life release date" for press/IMDb sources.
response.hop2_query: Mala Mala Train Life release years
hop2_docs: Mala vita | Mala vita (Wretched Life) is an opera in three acts composed by Umberto Giordano to a libretto by Nicola Daspuro adapted from Salvatore Di Giacomo and Goffredo Cognetti's verismo play of the same name.  Giordano's first full-length opera, "Mala vita" premiered on 21 February 1892 at the Teatro Argentina.  It was subsequently performed in Naples, Vienna, Berlin and Milan, and other Italian cities over the next two years.  In 1897 a considerably re-worked and revised version under the title "Il voto" (The Vow) premiered in Milan.  Within a few years both versions had disappeared from the repertoire.  Amongst its rare modern revivals was the 2002 performance at the Teatro Umberto Giordano in Foggia which was recorded live and released on the Bongiovanni label.

Mala (song) | "Mala" is the first single from Yolandita's 31st studio album, Mala.  This song was written by Maria Isabel Saavedra and produced by Jose Luis Pagan.  The Yolandita new single “Mala”, was released on October 6, 2008 on national radio.
Mala Mala Airport | Mala Mala Airport (IATA: AAM, ICAO: FAMD) is an airport near Mala Mala, South Africa.

Eskimo (film) | Eskimo (also known as Mala the Magnificent and Eskimo Wife-Traders) is a 1933 American Pre-Code drama film directed by W. S. Van Dyke and released by Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer (MGM).  It is based on the books "Der Eskimo" and "Die Flucht ins weisse Land" by Danish explorer and author Peter Freuchen.  The film stars Ray Mala as Mala, Lulu Wong Wing as Mala's first wife Aba, Lotus Long as Mala's second wife Iva, Peter Freuchen as the Ship Captain, W. S. Van Dyke as Inspector White, and Joseph Sauers as Sergeant Hunt.

Mala, Kerala | Mala is the name of a small town in Thrissur district of Kerala, south India.  There is a Jewish synagogue at Mala town .  At the moment it is in ruins.  The famous Pambu Mekkattu Mana (temple) is located here and attracts thousands of devotees every year.

Mala: I Mousiki Tou Anemou (song) | "Mala: I Mousiki Tou Anemou" (Greek: Μάλα: Η Μουσική του Ανέμου ; English: Mala: the music of the wind ) is a single by Anna Vissi from the original soundtrack , released in 2002 by Sony Music in Greece and Cyprus.
Mala Rodríguez | María Rodríguez Garrido, also known as La Mala, La Mala María, or Mala Rodríguez ("Mala", Spanish for "bad"), is a Latin Grammy Award-winning Spanish hip hop singer.

response.answer: Insufficient information to determine which came first.
